content: {"personas": [{"perspective": "Scientific", "name": "Dr. Krista Varady", "expertise": "Nutrition scientist and metabolic physiologist (University of Illinois Chicago) with two decades of human trial work on alternate-day fasting, time-restricted eating, and their effects on body composition and cardiometabolic biomarkers. Published primary RCTs and narrative reviews on IF. Her quantitative trial-based viewpoint is central because most mechanistic claims about IF require within-subject randomized evidence, not observational associations.", "question_focus": "Effect sizes from randomized controlled trials on weight, fat mass, appendicular lean mass, fasting glucose, insulin, HbA1c, LDL-C, triglycerides, blood pressure. Particularly interested in whether benefits exceed those of matched continuous energy restriction (isoenergetic comparisons), and whether adherence degrades after 6-12 months."}, {"perspective": "Regulatory", "name": "Dr. Andrea J. MacNeill", "expertise": "Regulatory-science physician with appointments at Health Canada Food Directorate and previous FDA Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition experience, specializing in health-claim evidence evaluation frameworks. Background in internal medicine + regulatory policy. Her viewpoint matters because intermittent fasting is marketed with wellness claims that do not fit the traditional drug benefit-risk framework but still fall under dietary guidance authority.", "question_focus": "Whether current evidence meets substantiation thresholds for any regulated health claim; EFSA Panel on Dietetic Products criteria for 'reduction of disease risk' claims; FDA qualified-health-claim standards; and safety signals (lean-mass loss, hypoglycemia in antidiabetic-drug users, eating-disorder precipitation) that warrant consumer advisories."}, {"perspective": "Industry", "name": "Mark Haub", "expertise": "Human-nutrition applied-science investigator with adjunct ties to commercial weight-management programs and prior consulting for meal-replacement and digital-health-coaching companies. His domain is translating dietary evidence into consumer products and behavioral-coaching programs. This viewpoint matters because the commercial IF ecosystem (apps, coaching, supplements) operates ahead of the evidence base and shapes lay understanding.", "question_focus": "Effectiveness and adherence of commercial TRE and ADF app interventions (Zero, Fastic, LIFE Fasting Tracker); dropout curves over 12-24 weeks; revenue-per-user and retention metrics; and how product claims compare to peer-reviewed effect sizes. Interested in real-world adherence data that academic trials underreport."}, {"perspective": "Economic", "name": "Dr. Alison Galvani", "expertise": "Health-policy modeler and mathematical epidemiologist (Yale) whose laboratory builds cost-effectiveness and population-simulation models for preventive interventions. Work includes Markov models for obesity-related disease pathways. Her viewpoint matters because any population-scale claim about IF benefit must translate into QALYs, ICERs, and downstream healthcare-cost displacement to be policy-actionable.", "question_focus": "Cost-per-QALY of IF counseling delivered in primary care vs. usual-care weight management; sensitivity to adherence decay; opportunity cost vs. other proven weight-management interventions (structured CER, pharmacotherapy); and who pays (insurer, employer, consumer) under different delivery models."}, {"perspective": "Public_Health", "name": "Dr. Katherine Zeratsky", "expertise": "Registered dietitian-nutritionist and clinical educator (Mayo Clinic) focused on population-level dietary guidance and counseling in primary-care and specialty clinics. Background in translating evidence into practice recommendations for diverse populations. Her viewpoint matters because IF is widely adopted by laypeople without clinical supervision and the public-health cost of generic recommendations can fall on populations least equipped to mitigate harms.", "question_focus": "Equity of IF applicability across socioeconomic strata, shift workers, pregnant/lactating individuals, adolescents, and adults with comorbidities (T1DM, T2DM on sulfonylureas/insulin, CKD). Also adverse-event reporting under real-world (not RCT) conditions, and clear communication of what evidence does and does not support."}]}
